name: sangaboard

metadata.name: sangaboard
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3)
metadata.summary: Communication to the Sangaboard unipolar motor driver

documentation:
pySangaboard
============================
This is the python module that communicates to the Sangaboard v0.3, an open-source board for driving unipolar stepper motors. It also supports older versions of the Sangaboard that were  based on an Arduino Nano and a couple of Darlington pair ICs.

Contributors
============
Richard Bowman (University of Bath, UK) Wrote the initial version of the library when the board was specific to the OpenFlexure Microscope.
Julian Stirling (University of Bath, UK) Contributed to the initial library, and divided to code base to make an independent sangaboard library
Boyko Vodenicharski and Filip Ayazi (University of Cambridge, UK) contributed Python 3 support, endstop support.

This project is (c) 2017 by the contributors, and released GNU GPL v3.0 (software).


Install
=========
You can install pySangaboard with the following pip command

pip install sangaboard

Documentation
=============

The documentation is available on `Read the Docs <https://sangaboard.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html>`__

Developer notes
===============

Publishing
++++++++++

* `pip install twine`
* `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel`
* `twine check dist/*`
* `twine upload dist/*`

modules.name: sangaboard.sangaboard
documentation:
Sangaboard module

This Python code allows control of the Sangaboard

It is (c) Richard Bowman & Julian Stirling 2019 and released under GNU GPL v3
!/usr/bin/python
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.__init__
documentation:
Create a sangaboard object.

Arguments are passed to the constructor of
{class}`Sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument`,
most likely the only one necessary is `port` which should be set to the serial port
you will use to communicate with the motor controller. That's the first argument so
it doesn't need to be named.

Note that the list of port settings is only used when autodetecting a port. If you
want to use an alternative baud rate, for example, it can be passed as an additional
keyword argument.

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.test_communications
documentation: Overrides superclass, used in self.open(), and port scanning

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.move_rel
documentation:
Make a relative move.

displacement: integer or array/list of 3 integers
axis: None (for 3-axis moves) or one of 'x','y','z'

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.release_motors
documentation: De-energise the stepper motor coils

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.zero_position
documentation: Set the current position to zero

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.move_abs
documentation:
Make an absolute move to a position

NB the sangaboard only accepts relative move commands, so this first
queries the board for its position, then instructs it to make about
relative move.

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.query
documentation: Send a message and read the response.  See ExtensibleSerialInstrument.query()

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.list_modules
documentation:
Return a list of strings detailing optional modules.

Each module will correspond to a string of the form ``Module Name: Model``

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Sangaboard.print_help
documentation: Print the stage's built-in help message.

Class managing serial communications with a Sangaboard

The `Sangaboard` class handles setting up communications with the sangaboard,
wraps the various serial commands in Python methods, and provides iterators and
context managers to simplify opening/closing the hardware connection and some
other tasks like conducting a linear scan.

Arguments to the constructor are passed to the constructor of
{class}`Sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument`,
most likely the only one necessary is `port` which should be set to the serial port
you will use to communicate with the motor controller.

This class can be used as a context manager, i.e. it's encouraged to use it as:

```
with Sangaboard() as sb:
    sb.move_rel([1000,0,0])
```

In that case, the serial port will automatically be closed at the end of the block,
even if an error occurs. Otherwise, be sure to call the
{meth}`~.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.close()` method to release the serial port.

classes.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.LightSensor

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.LightSensor.gain
documentation:
"Get or set the current gain value of the light sensor.

Valid gain values are defined in the `valid_gains` property, and should be floating-point numbers.

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.LightSensor.__get_gain_values
documentation:
Read the allowable values for the light sensor's gain.

This function will attempt to return a list of floating-point numbers which may
be used as values of the `gain` property.  If the stage returns non-floating-point
values, the list will be of strings.

An optional module giving access to the light sensor.

If a light sensor is enabled in the motor controller's firmware, then
the {class}`sangaboard.Sangaboard` will gain an optional
module which is an instance of this class. It can be used to access
the light sensor (usually via the I2C bus).

classes.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Endstops

methods.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Endstops.home
documentation:
Home given/all axes in the given direction (min/max/both)

```
param direction

one of {min,max,both}

param axes

list of axes e.g. ['x','y']
```

class_variables.name: sangaboard.sangaboard.Endstops.installed
documentation: List of installed endstop types (min, max, soft)

An optional module for use with endstops.

If endstops are installed in the firmware the {class}`sangaboard.Sangaboard`
will gain an optional module which is an instance of this class. It can be used to retrieve
the type, state of the endstops, read and write maximum positions, and home.

modules.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument
documentation:
This module has been modified from the chopped-out of class from [nplab].
It is a serial instrument class to simplify the process of interfacing with
equipment that talks on a serial port. The idea is that your instrument can
subclass {class}`ExtensibleSerialInstrument` and provide methods to control the
hardware, which will mostly consist of `self.query()` commands. It also has
options for adding OptionalModules so that you don't have to have multiple
classes for lots of instruments with different configurations

The {class}`QueriedProperty` class is a convenient shorthand to create a property
that is read and/or set with a single serial query (i.e. a read followed by a write).

% module_author: Richard Rowman (c) 2017, released under GNU GPL

[nplab]: http://www.github.com/nanophotonics/nplab
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.list_serial_ports
documentation:
List available serial ports, including a workaround for Raspberry Pi

The built-in list of com ports misses the RPi hardware serial port.
This workaround explicitly checks for it, and includes it if present.

classes.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.__init__
documentation: Set up the serial port and so on.
this list ensures the are all tried during autodetection

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.open
documentation:
Open communications with the serial port.

If no port is specified, it will attempt to autodetect.  If quiet=True
then we don't warn when ports are opened multiple times.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.close
documentation: Cleanly close the device. Includes proper logging statements.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.__del__
documentation: Emergency close the device. Try to avoid having to use this.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.__exit__
documentation: Cleanly close down the instrument at end of a with block.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.write
documentation: Write a string to the serial port

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.flush_input_buffer
documentation: Make sure there's nothing waiting to be read, and clear the buffer if there is.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.readline
documentation: Read one line from the serial port.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.communications_lock
documentation: A lock object used to protect access to the communications bus

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.read_multiline
documentation:
Read one line from the underlying bus.  Must be overriden.

This should not need to be reimplemented unless there's a more efficient
way of reading multiple lines than multiple calls to readline().

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.query
documentation:
Write a string to the stage controller and return its response.

It will block until a response is received.  The multiline and termination_line commands
will keep reading until a termination phrase is reached.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.parsed_query
documentation:
Perform a query, returning a parsed form of the response.

First query the instrument with the given query string, then compare
the response against a template.  The template may contain text and
placeholders (e.g. %i and %f for integer and floating point values
respectively).  Regular expressions are also allowed - each group is
considered as one item to be parsed.  However, currently it's not
supported to use both % placeholders and regular expressions at the
same time.

If placeholders %i, %f, etc. are used, the returned values are
automatically converted to integer or floating point, otherwise you
must specify a parsing function (applied to all groups) or a list of
parsing functions (applied to each group in turn).

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.int_query
documentation: Perform a query and return the result(s) as integer(s) (see parsedQuery)

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.float_query
documentation: Perform a query and return the result(s) as float(s) (see parsedQuery)

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.test_communications
documentation:
Check if the device is available on the current port.

This should be overridden by subclasses.  Assume the port has been
successfully opened and the settings are as defined by self.port_settings.
Usually this function sends a command and checks for a known reply.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.find_port
documentation:
Find the first serial port where we can establish communications.

Iterate through the available serial ports and query them to see
if our instrument is there.  If a list of settings is provided in
`self.port_settings_list` we will try each configuration in turn.

class_variables.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.termination_character
documentation: : All messages to or from the instrument end with this character.

class_variables.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.termination_line
documentation: : If multi-line responses are recieved, they must end with this string

class_variables.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.ExtensibleSerialInstrument.port_settings_list
documentation: : If multiple different settings are possible,

An instrument that communicates by sending strings back and forth over serial

This base class provides commonly-used mechanisms that support the use of
serial instruments.  Most interactions with this class involve
a call to the `query` method.  This writes a message and returns the reply.
This has been hacked together from the nplab_ MessageBusInstrument and SerialInstrument
classes.

**Threading Notes**

The message bus protocol includes a property, `communications_lock`.  All
commands that use the communications bus should be protected by this lock.
It's also permissible to use it to protect sequences of calls to the bus
that must be atomic (e.g. a multi-part exchange of messages).  However, try
not to hold it too long - or odd things might happen if other threads are
blocked for a long time.  The lock is reentrant so there's no issue with
acquiring it twice.

classes.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.OptionalModule

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.OptionalModule.confirm_available
documentation: Check if module is available, no return, will raise exception if not available!

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.OptionalModule.describe
documentation: Consistently spaced desciption for listing modules

This allows a `ExtensibleSerialInstrument` to have optional features.

OptionalModule is designed as a base class
for interfacing with optional modules which may or may not be included with
the serial instrument, and can be added or removed at run-time.

classes.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.QueriedProperty

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.QueriedProperty.__get__
documentation: TODO: standardise the return (single value only vs parsed result), consider bool

A Property interface that reads and writes from the instrument on the bus.

This returns a property-like (i.e. a descriptor) object. You can use it
in a class definition just like a property. The property it creates will
interact with the instrument over the communication bus to set and retrieve
its value. It uses calls to `ExtensibleSerialInstrument.parsed_query` to set or
get the value of the property.

`QueriedProperty` can be used to define properties on a `ExtensibleSerialInstrument`
or an `OptionalModule` (in which case the `ExtensibleSerialInstrument.parsed_query`
method of the parent object will be used).

Arguments:

```
get_cmd

the string sent to the instrument to obtain the value

set_cmd

the string used to set the value (use {} or % placeholders)

validate

a list of allowable values

valrange

a maximum and minimum value

fdel

a function to call when it's deleted

doc

the docstring

response_string

supply a % code (as you would for response_string in a
ExtensibleSerialInstrument.parsed_query)

ack_writes

set to "readline" to discard a line of input after writing.
```

classes.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.write
documentation:
Accept data being written to the port

This is data being sent out of the serial port, and is
stored in the `write buffer`.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.inWaiting
documentation:
Return the number of bytes in the input buffer

NB the input buffer is for bytes being read from the port

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.flushInput
documentation:
Flush the input buffer, so there are no previously-arrived
bytes when we read a response.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.register_response
documentation:
Match a query and respond to it with a function.

See `respond_to` for details of how this works.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.remove_response
documentation:
Remove a previously registered response

The regular expression string must be exactly the same as the
one you are trying to remove.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.replace_response
documentation:
Remove a response and replace it with another.

Order is not preserved.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.respond_to
documentation:
Do something in response to a message.

Each time we receive a termination character, we attempt to match
the message (which doesn't include the termination character) with
the regular expressions in the `responses` property. The first
one that matches is used.

The regular expression match object's `groups()` value is passed
to the function, which can then use it to return its response.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.readline
documentation: Emulate reading a line from the serial port.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.read
documentation: Emulate reading some bytes from the serial port.

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.open
documentation: Open the serial port (currently does nothing)

methods.name: sangaboard.extensible_serial_instrument.DummySerialDevice.close
documentation: Close the serial port (currently does nothing)

This class exists to allow unit testing without serial port hardware.

It can be passed to an `ExtensibleSerialInstrument` in place of the
serial port name, and will emulate a device using stored responses.

It works on a line-by-line basis, so it will only do anything once
it gets a termination character (by default, newline). It tries to give
the same interface as `pyserial.Serial` at least as far as the
methods used by `ExtensibleSerialInstrument` go.

The `timeout` property will be used to cause delays if there is
no response to give - but infinite timeouts raise an exception rather
than attempt to do anything clever with threads.

You can set `print_buffers` to True in order to make it print the
input and output buffers each time the "port" is written to or read
from.
